nameMeanings.name: de
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Dilip, Anirban, Gopal, Alok, Anindya, Arunava, Asit, Asok, Dibyendu, Goutam, Indra, Indranil.
Indian: variant of   Dey  .

variants.name: Dey
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Ashit, Indu, Naina, Partha, Soumya, Ajit, Anjan, Anjana, Anup, Arjun, Arun, Ashim.
English, Scottish, and Welsh: variant of   Day  .
German: topographic name from a field name in North Rhine-Westphalia, denoting a sizeable piece of land, from Middle Low German dē ‘thigh’ (referring to the shape of the property).

nameMeanings.name: Mowbray,
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English (northern; of Norman origin): habitational name from Montbray (Manche). Robert de Mowbray was Earl of Northumbria 1086–95, but was later imprisoned and deposed for rebelling against William II.

variants.name: Mulberry
meanings: English (Middlesex and Cambridgeshire): variant of   Mowbray  .
Irish (Derry): Anglicized form of Gaelic Ó Maoilbhearaigh ‘descendant of the devotee of (Saint) Bearach’.

variants.name: Moubray
meanings: English: variant of   Mowbray  .

variants.name: Mayberry
meanings: English: most probably an altered form of   Mowbray  . It is also found as   Maybury  , which has the form of an English habitational name. There is a place near Woking in Surrey so called; however, this is not recorded until 1885 and is probably derived from the surname. In England this surname is found mainly in the West Midlands; it has also spread into Wales. In Ireland this form is common in Ulster; MacLysaght records that it was taken there from England in the 17th century.

variants.name: Memory
meanings: English: variant of   Mowbray  .

nameMeanings.name: Baron
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: French, English, Dutch, Polish, Czech, Romanian, and Croatian; Breton (also Le Baron): from Old French barun, baron, Middle English barun, baroun, Middle Dutch baroen, Polish, Czech, Romanian, and dialectal Croatian baron ‘baron’ (i.e. a nobleman ranking below an earl and above a knight), a title of nobility which was in England also awarded to certain freemen of the cities of London and York and of the Cinque Ports. As a surname it is unlikely to be a status name denoting a person of rank. Generally, it referred to service in a baronial household or was acquired as a nickname by a peasant who had ideas above his station or applied to a man showing characteristics of a nobleman. See also 3 and 4 below, compare Barron and Lebaron .
French: habitational name from (Le) Baron, the name of several places in various parts of France. Compare   Lebaron  .
German: status name for a freeman or baron, from barūn ‘imperial or church official’, a loanword in Middle High German from Old French (see 1 above).

variants.name: Barron
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Jose, Juan, Jesus, Manuel, Francisco, Carlos, Miguel, Raul, Pedro, Ruben, Alfredo, Luis.

Scottish: status name from Scots baron, which in the Highlands denoted a small proprietary landholder. In Angus it alluded specifically to a tenant of one of the small baronies attached to the Abbey of Coupar Angus. The surname was taken by Scottish migrants to Ulster. Compare Baron .
Scottish: shortened form of McBarron, from Scottish Gaelic Mac a' Bharain ‘son of the baron’. See Baron and 1 above.

variants.name: Boron
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Polish Krystyna, Edyta, Leszek, Mieczyslaw, Mieczyslawa, Wladyslaw.
Polish (Boroń): from a pet form of the personal names Borzysław or Borzymir, formed with the element bor ‘to fight’ (from Old Slavic boriti se).
French: habitational name from a place so called in Franche-Comté.

variants.name: Baro
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Mario, Manuel, Ana, Angel, Carlos, Cesar, Fernando, Jesus, Jose, Orlando, Ramon, Rosalia.  Italian Guido, Angelo, Carmelo, Federico, Lucio.
Catalan (Baró): status name from baró ‘free man, baron’ (see Baron 1), or a habitational name from a place so named.
Hungarian (Báró): status name from báró ‘baron’. In the form Baró, it could be from a pet form of the personal names Barabás (see Barabas ) or Bertalan.

variants.name: Barona
meanings: Spanish: from the feminine form of Barón ‘baron’ (see Baron ), applied as a nickname or as an occupational name for a member of the household of a baroness.
Italian: probably from the feminine form of   Barone  . The name is concentrated in Sondrio province (Lombardy).

variants.name: Barren
meanings: English: probably a variant of   Baron   or   Barran  .

nameMeanings.name: of
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Chinese Young, Sung, Jung, Yong, Chang, Kwang, Sang, Dong, Hyun, Jae, Jin, Kyung, Jong, Chong, Chung, Byung, Jeong, Pyong, Myong, Hak, Jin Hwan, Sook, Yeong, Choon.
Korean: variant of   O  .
Japanese: variant of Ō (see O ).

variants.name: O
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Chinese Young, Bo Young, Chae, Chong Hwan, Dong Kun, Ho, Hwan, Hyun, Jong, Jung, Ki Soon, Kwang.  Korean Min, Chong, Chol, Jung Ho, Myong, Sa, Sung Kyun.

Korean (오; Chinese character: 吳): historically, there were two Chinese characters for the O surname, but by the late Koryŏ period (14th century AD ), one of them had disappeared. Some records indicate that there are as many as 210 different clans for the remaining character, but only sixteen can be positively documented. All sixteen of these clans originate from the same ancestor, O Ch’ŏm (吳 瞻), who migrated to Korea from China during the reign of Shilla's King Chijŭng (500–514 AD ). O is a common surname throughout Korea. Compare Oh .

nameMeanings.name: Thirsh,
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Jewish Chaim, Emanuel, Gedaliah, Hyman, Myer.
Jewish (Ashkenazic): variant of   Hirsch  .
Americanized form of German   Hirsch  .

variants.name: Hirsch
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Jewish Emanuel, Meyer, Shimon, Moshe, Yosef, Beril, Chaim, Elihu, Eliyohu.  German Kurt, Otto, Erwin, Hans, Heinz, Helmut, Beate, Fritz, Gunther, Hedwig, Hilde, Ilse.

German: from Middle High German hir(t)z ‘deer, stag’; a metonymic occupational name for a keeper of deer, a nickname for someone thought to resemble a deer or stag, or a topographic or habitational name referring to a house distinguished by the sign of a stag. It is also found in some central European countries, for example in Czechia and Slovenia, often as a translation into German of corresponding Slavic surnames, e.g. Jelen . In some cases this is also a standardized form of the Gottscheerish (i.e. Gottschee German) surname Hiris .

Jewish (Ashkenazic): from the Yiddish male personal name Hirsh ‘deer’, which is common because of the association of the deer with the Hebrew personal name Naphtali, deriving from the blessing by Jacob of his sons (Genesis 49: 21), in which Naphtali is referred to as a ‘hind let loose’. Compare Deer , Jelen , Jelinek , and Jelonek .

nameMeanings.name: Surety
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of Family Names in Britain and Ireland © University of the West of England 2016
meanings: English: possibly a re-etymologized form of   Surtees  . Compare Ridley Surtees, 1797 in   IGI   (Saint Mary at Hill, London), and Richard Surtees, 1787 in   IGI   (Holborn, Middx).

variants.name: Surtees
meanings: English (Durham and Northumberland): habitational name from an unidentified place, probably an Anglo-Norman French name for Upper Teesdale, composed of the Old French prefix sur (from Latin super) ‘upper’ + the English river name Te(i)se, Tees (of uncertain etymology).

variants.name: IGI
meanings: English: variant of   Wellings  . Compare Edward Wellings, 1662, Humphrey Wellens, 1684 in   IGI   (Wentnor, Shrops).

nameMeanings.name: of
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Chinese Young, Sung, Jung, Yong, Chang, Kwang, Sang, Dong, Hyun, Jae, Jin, Kyung, Jong, Chong, Chung, Byung, Jeong, Pyong, Myong, Hak, Jin Hwan, Sook, Yeong, Choon.
Korean: variant of   O  .
Japanese: variant of Ō (see O ).

variants.name: O
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Chinese Young, Bo Young, Chae, Chong Hwan, Dong Kun, Ho, Hwan, Hyun, Jong, Jung, Ki Soon, Kwang.  Korean Min, Chong, Chol, Jung Ho, Myong, Sa, Sung Kyun.

Korean (오; Chinese character: 吳): historically, there were two Chinese characters for the O surname, but by the late Koryŏ period (14th century AD ), one of them had disappeared. Some records indicate that there are as many as 210 different clans for the remaining character, but only sixteen can be positively documented. All sixteen of these clans originate from the same ancestor, O Ch’ŏm (吳 瞻), who migrated to Korea from China during the reign of Shilla's King Chijŭng (500–514 AD ). O is a common surname throughout Korea. Compare Oh .

nameMeanings.name: Magna
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Jose, Mario, Raymundo, Manuel, Manolo, Orlando, Sixto, Alfredo, Ana, Andres, Angel, Armando.  Italian Carlo, Angelo, Alessandro, Antonio, Cesare, Ciriaco, Enrico, Evangelio, Gaetano, Gilda, Giulio, Luigi.
Italian:
from the personal name Magno, from Latin Magnus (see Magnus 1).

variants.name: Magnotta
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Rocco, Angelo, Mario, Alphonso, Amadeo, Amedeo, Antonio, Emilio, Gerardo, Livio, Luigi, Ugo.
Italian (southern): from a diminutive of Magna, a feminine form of Magno 1.

variants.name: Magni
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Riccardo, Tiziano, Angelo, Eliseo, Enrico, Enzo, Evo.
Italian: patronymic or plural form of   Magno  .

variants.name: Magnus
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Gerhard, Bernd, Egon, Erwin, Kurt, Otto, Reinhard, Volker, Wilhelm, Wolfgang.

German, Flemish, and Walloon: from the personal name Magnus, which is from the Latin adjective magnus ‘great’ or a Latinized form of a short form of ancient Germanic compound names based on the element magan ‘strength, might’. The name was borne by several early Christian saints, including St. Magnus (8th century), a monk in St. Gallen in Switzerland and missionary in Allgäu in southern Germany, whose real name was Magnoald. This surname is also found in France (Alsace and Lorraine). In Belgium, it may also be a Latinized (humanistic) form of Walloon Legrand .

English, Scottish, Swedish, Danish, Norwegian, and North German: from the Scandinavian personal name Magnus, borne by Magnus the Good (died 1047), king of Norway and Denmark, who was named for the Emperor Charlemagne, Latin Carolus Magnus ‘Charles the Great’, under the impression that Magnus was a personal name. The name spread from Norway to the eastern Scandinavian royal houses, and became popular all over Scandinavia and thence in the English Danelaw. It has also been adopted as a Jewish name.

variants.name: Magnotti
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Vito, Angelo.
Italian: patronymic or plural form of Magnotto, a pet form of Magno .

variants.name: Magnone
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Caesar, Carmine, Giuseppe, Angelo, Bonaventura, Dino, Domenic, Fiore, Guido, Matteo, Natale, Rinaldo.
Italian:
from Magno + the augmentative suffix -one.

nameMeanings.name: Carta
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Salvatore, Attilio, Adolfo, Alvaro, Angelo, Antonio, Armando, Carmello, Donatella, Filomena, Francesco, Georgio, Giuseppa, Luciano, Luigi.  Spanish Carlos, Julio, Salvador, Angel, Elodia, Jesus.
Italian (Sardinia and Sicily): metonymic occupational name for a notary or administrator, from carta ‘document’.
Spanish: nickname from carta ‘letter’.

variants.name: Cartelli
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Salvatore, Antonino, Liliana, Vincenza.
Italian: patronymic or plural form of a diminutive of   Carta  .

nameMeanings.name: William
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Probably the most successful of all the Old French names of Germanic origin that were introduced to England by the Normans. It is derived from Germanic wil ‘will, desire’ + helm ‘helmet, protection’. The fact that it was borne by the Conqueror himself does not seem to have inhibited its favour with the ‘conquered’ population: in the first century after the Conquest it was the commonest male name of all, and not only among the Normans. In the later Middle Ages it was overtaken by John , but continued to run second to that name until the 20th century, when the picture became more fragmented.

historicEvents.title: Magna Carta
historicEvents.description: King John of England agreed to a charter of rights called the Magna Carta. The document was originally drafted by the Archbishop of Canterbury to establish peace between King John and a group of rebel barons. The Magna Carta was completed in 1215 and is now considered to be one of the greatest constitutional documents of all time.

historicEvents.title: France Declares War on England
historicEvents.description: France declared war on England as Louis VIII of France attacked Poitou and northern Gascony.

historicEvents.title: 100 Years of Conflict Between England and France Ends
historicEvents.description: Treaty of Paris was signed on December 4, 1259, which ended 100 years of conflict between England and France.

historicEvents.title: Jews Expelled From England
historicEvents.description: After years of anti-Semitic behavior, King Edward I expelled Jews from England in 1290.

en.lifeSummary: William de Mowbray, Baron of Thirsh, Surety of Magna Carta was born in 1208, in England. He had at least 1 son with Avice d’Aubigny. He died in 1293, in his hometown, at the age of 85.

fr.lifeSummary: William de Mowbray, Baron of Thirsh, Surety of Magna Carta est né en 1208, à Angleterre. Il a eu au moins 1 fils avec Avice d’Aubigny. Il est décédé en 1293, dans sa ville natale, à l’âge de 85 ans.

de.lifeSummary: William de Mowbray, Baron of Thirsh, Surety of Magna Carta wurde 1208 in England geboren. Er hatte mit Avice d’Aubigny mindestens 1 Sohn. Er starb 1293 im Alter von 85 Jahren in seinem Geburtsort.

it.lifeSummary: William de Mowbray, Baron of Thirsh, Surety of Magna Carta è nato il nel 1208 nel England. Lui avuto almeno 1 figlio con Avice d’Aubigny. Morì nel 1293, nel sua cittadina, all'età di 85 anni.

ru.lifeSummary: William de Mowbray, Baron of Thirsh, Surety of Magna Carta родился в 1208 г. в местности Англия. У него был по крайней мере 1 сын от Avice d’Aubigny. Он умер в 1293 г. в его родном городе в возрасте 85 лет.

es.lifeSummary: William de Mowbray, Baron of Thirsh, Surety of Magna Carta nació en 1208, en Inglaterra. Tuvo por lo menos 1 hijo con Avice d’Aubigny. Murió en 1293, en su pueblo, a la edad de 85 años.
